What it means to represent the United States:
Having these three letters across my chest bring so much honor, respect, and gratitude. Knowing how proud my family is of me for reaching my goals is the best feeling in the world. I hope to be able to continue to strive in order to make my wife, children and family proud with everything that I do.

bio.fun_fact: Ranks in the top 10 in NCAA women’s hockey in career points, goals and assists
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Position: Forward
  • Daughter of 18-year NHL star, Bobby Carpenter
  • Mom, Julie, is a former competitive figure skater
  • Brother, Robert “Bobo”, played college hockey at Boston University
  • Brother, Brendan, played college football at Endicott College (Beverly, Mass.) 
  • Ranks in the top 10 in NCAA women’s hockey in career points, goals and assists
Biographical Information

Alex Carpenter was born into a hockey family. Her father, Bobby Carpenter, is an 18-year NHL veteran who was the first U.S.-born player to score 50 goals in a single season. Carpenter grew up playing hockey in New Jersey, where her dad was an assistant coach with the Devils, and after the NHL team practiced, she would take a few shots on goal at NHL Hall of Fame goalie Martin Brodeur. At age 10,  she wrote an essay saying she would be an Olympian, and as Carpenter remembers: “I couldn't even spell Olympics at that time. I think I put a K in it."

Carpenter attended Boston College to play college hockey and left the school as the career leader in points, goals and assists. Carpenter made her IIHF World Championship debut for the U.S. women’s hockey team at the 2013 IIHF Women’s World Championships and has played in 10 world championships, being named the best forward at the 2024 IIHF Women’s World Championships.

Carpenter has also represented the U.S. at four Four Nations Cups, and her selection for the Olympic Winter Games Milano Cortina 2026 will mark her third Olympic team. Carpenter was the first overall pick in the 2015 NWHL Draft, and after one season playing for the Boston Pride, she spent four seasons playing in China. She has since returned to play in North America and now plays professionally in PWHL for the Seattle Torrent. 

In 2023, Carpenter married Steph Klein, one of the first female equipment managers in men’s hockey who worked in both the KHL and AHL.  

Olympic Experience
  • 3-time Olympian; 3-time Olympic medalist (1 gold, 2 silver)
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, gold (Team - Women)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, silver (Team - Women)
    • Olympic Games Sochi 2014, silver (Team - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – gold (Team - Women)
  • Years of participation: Team - Women 2013, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2019, 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025
  • Medals: 10 (7 gold, 3 silver)
    • Gold – 2025 (Team - Women); 2023 (Team - Women); 2019 (Team - Women); 2017 (Team - Women); 2016 (Team - Women); 2015 (Team - Women); 2013 (Team - Women)
    • Silver – 2024 (Team - Women); 2022 (Team - Women); 2021 (Team - Women)

bio.education: Brandywine Heights High School ‘12 and DeVry University
bio.biography:
Quick Facts

  • Specialty: Giant Slalom, Standing Slalom and Standing

  • Son of Edward and Suzanne Carter

  • He has one brother, Jackson

  • Born with Fibular Hemimelia and had his right leg amputated as an infant 

  • Began skiing at age eight through the Pennsylvania Center for Adapted Sports

  • Serves as an Athlete Mentor for Classroom Champions, a non-profit organization partnering Olympic and Paralympic athletes with students and teachers in underprivileged communities, and is a motivational speaker

  • Enjoys yoga, hiking and watching movies

  • All-around athlete, participating in basketball, swimming, tennis, dance and karate as a child

Biographical Information

Tyler Carter is a retired Paralympic alpine skier known for his resilience and dedication to Para sports. Born in Reading, Pennsylvania, and raised in Topton, he had his right leg amputated below the knee as an infant after being born without a fibula. Carter discovered skiing at age eight through the Pennsylvania Center for Adapted Sports, quickly developing a passion for the sport.

He made his Paralympic debut at the Paralympic Winter Games Sochi 2014, competing in the giant slalom. Carter went on to represent Team USA in the Paralympic Winter Games PyeongChang 2018 and the Paralympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, participating in multiple alpine skiing events. In recognition of his leadership and commitment, he was selected as a flag bearer for Team USA during the Opening Ceremonies of the Paralympic Winter Games Beijing 2022.

Beyond his athletic achievements, Carter is a strong advocate for healthy and active lifestyles. He serves as an Athlete Mentor for Classroom Champions, a nonprofit organization that partners Olympic and Paralympic athletes with students and teachers in underserved communities to inspire and motivate the next generation. Off the slopes, Carter enjoys yoga, hiking and watching movies. He also acts as a motivational speaker, sharing his passions and journey.

Paralympic Experience
  • Three-time Paralympian (2014, 2018, 2022)
  • Paralympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, 31st (downhill), 33rd (giant slalom), DNF (super-G), DNF (super combined)
  • Paralympic Winter Games PyeongChang 2018, 19th (slalom)
  • Paralympic Winter Games Sochi 2014, 27th (giant slalom)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2015 – 14th (slalom), 24th (giant slalom)
  • Years of Participation: 2015
  • Top Finish: 2015 – 14th (slalom)

HEIGHT 6'0" WEIGHT 148 lbs

Follow Carter on Twitter @KindrickUSA, on Instagram @Kindrick1 and on Facebook by searching "Kindrick Carter"!

Kindrick Carter was born on Thursday, October 7, 1999 in Rapid City, S.D. The native of Warrington, Pa. was first introduced to the sport of skeleton during the 2014 Sochi Winter Olympic Games. It took him only 20 seconds to realize that skeleton was the sport for him. Less than two years later, he is a hopeful for the 2016 Youth Olympic Games in Lillehammer, Norway.

Carter is currently a sophomore at the 21st Century Cyber Charter High School in Warrington. He loves speed. Whether it's being the fastest kid on the playground, to being the first to finish a math quiz, speed is his obsession. His other interests include snowboarding, piano, music, surfing and working with engines. One day, he hopes to design and build cars of the future.


FUN FACTS

- He once built a go kart, which he promptly sold for money to use for a car.
- He plans to attend college for automotive engineering.
- He played the trumpet for five years. He now plays the piano.
- He started going to Linkin' Park concerts when he was 10 years old.
- He enjoys music and his favorite band is Flux Pavillion.

CAREER HIGHLIGHTS

  • 2015-2016 Youth Olympic Games Qualification Series in skeleton - SILVER in Lake Placid #1 (10/15); GOLD in Lake Placid #2 (10/15)

Results:

Quick Facts
  • Son of Don and Tracy Cash
  • Has three brothers, Donny, James and Mike
  • Hobbies include camping, hiking, tennis, working out and traveling
  • Is a licensed banker
  • Makes his own hockey sticks
  • Inducted into the USOPC Hall of Fame, Class of 2025
Biographical Information

Steve Cash is a legendary figure in sled hockey, celebrated as a three-time Paralympic gold medalist and five-time world champion. Widely regarded as one of the greatest goaltenders in the sport’s history, Cash made history at the Paralympic Winter Games Vancouver 2010 by becoming the only goalie ever to record five shutouts in a single tournament. He holds virtually every major goaltending record in sled hockey, both for Team USA and internationally, including most minutes played, highest save percentage, and the second-lowest goals-against average.

Off the ice, Cash continues to shape the future of the sport through coaching and mentorship. He has developed specialized training programs for sled hockey goaltenders and remains deeply committed to nurturing the next generation of athletes. He is a passionate advocate for mental health, working to break down the stigma surrounding psychological challenges among elite athletes.

Paralympic Experience
  • 4-time Paralympian; 4-time Paralympic medalist (3 gold, 1 bronze)
    • Paralympic Games Pyeongchang 2018, gold (Team - Mixed)
    • Paralympic Games Sochi 2014, gold (Team - Mixed)
    • Paralympic Games Vancouver 2010, gold (Team - Mixed)
    • Paralympic Games Turin 2006, bronze (Team - Men)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2012 – gold (Team - Men)
  • Years of participation: Team - Men 2008, 2009, 2012
  • Medals: 3 (2 gold, 1 bronze)
    • Gold – 2012 (Team - Men); 2009 (Team - Men)
    • Bronze – 2008 (Team - Men)

Quick Facts
  • Specialty: Women's Giant Slalom, Super-G, Downhill
  • Daughter of John and Christy Cashman
  • Has two siblings, Jordan and Noah
  • Hails from a town with a population of 86
  • Serves as a barista at her family’s coffee shop
  • Plays the guitar
  • Hobbies include road biking, paddle boarding, playing basketball, hiking, camping, hunting, playing guitar, drawing and baking
Biographical Information

Keely Cashman learned to ski at Dodge Ridge, a small family-owned resort where her father coached her and her two brothers. She credits her father as her biggest inspiration, noting he taught her everything about the sport. 

Cashman's talent emerged early. In 2014, she competed as the only U-16 American woman at the second annual Future Ski Champions event. Two years later, she represented the United States at the Youth Olympic Winter Games Lillehammer 2016. Her progression continued as she earned her first U.S. Ski Team nomination to the Development Team for the 2018 season.

In January 2021, Cashman suffered a season-ending crash during super-G training at Garmisch-Partenkirchen, Germany. She spent eight days hospitalized with the accident causing a traumatic brain injury, concussion, strained MCL, hematomas in both hips and temporary loss of feeling in her foot. Remarkably, she returned to training that spring, though another crash at Mammoth Mountain in May resulted in severe facial cuts. Her determination to overcome these setbacks demonstrated the mental toughness that defines her career.

Cashman made her Olympic debut at the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, where she finished as the top American in downhill with a 17th-place finish and placed 27th in super-G. The 2024-25 season marked a breakthrough as she scored her best world cup results to date, highlighted by a sixth-place finish in the Garmisch super-G and a fifth-place finish in Tarvisio. 

Beyond skiing, Cashman participates in the annual Yosemite Facelift, volunteering to pick up trash throughout the national park. Her family opened The Serene Bean coffee shop in Pinecrest in 1998, with Keely working as a barista during the offseason. 

Olympic Experience
  • 2-time Olympian
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, 15th (Super G - Women, Team Alpine Combined - Women)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, (Super Combined - Women), 17th (Downhill - Women), 27th (Super G - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – 24th (Super G - Women)
  • Years of participation: Super G - Women 2025
  • Top finish: 24th – 2025 (Super G - Women)

bio.fun_fact: His father, Jeff, got him into the sport of curling
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Position: Skip
  • Speciality: Men's Curling
  • Diagnosed with autoimmune Guillain-Barré Syndrome- has struggled at times with walking and use of his hands because of the disease
Biographical Information 

Danny Casper was born and raised in Briarcliff Manor, New York and  starting curling at the age of 11 at Ardsley Curling Club (Ardsley-On-Hudson, NY). After his father, Jeff, got him into the sport, he was tutored by former U.S. national champion Bill Stopera, who lived in the area and also coached Casper’s future national team teammate at the same curling club, his son, Andrew.

Casper would skip the U.S. team at the 2023 FISU World University Games in Lake Placid where the team would win a silver medal. It was the U.S.'s second medal all-time at the Winter World University Games in men’s curling, joining a gold medal won in 2007, by a team with John Shuster as the skip.

Following the World University Games medal, Casper’s team would finish with two second-place finishes at the national championships (2023, 2025) and on the professional circuit with two Grand Slam of Curling Tier 2 wins in their first three professional seasons.

Casper missed a portion of the 2024-2025 curling season after being diagnosed with autoimmune disease, Guillain-Barré Syndrome. Some of the effects of the disease have caused Casper to struggle at times to walk and use his hands. His teammates will assist him with cleaning his rock on the ice if he is having trouble with his hands during the curling competition. 

Danny Casper graduated from the University of Minnesota with a degree in strategic communications. He stayed in Minnesota after college, working as a business development consultant and training at the USA Curling National Training Center in Chaska.
